title: OpenAI unveils Jalapeño, its first custom inference chip, with Broadcom
subtitle: A reticle-sized ASIC co-designed with Broadcom in a nine-month tape-out cycle, aimed at running ChatGPT inference more cheaply per watt starting late 2026.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: OpenAI and Broadcom announced Jalapeño, OpenAI's first custom Intelligence Processor — a reticle-sized ASIC engineered specifically for large-language-model inference. The two companies disclosed that the chip went from initial design to manufacturing tape-out in nine months, which they describe as the fastest ASIC cycle ever achieved in high-performance silicon. Initial deployment is targeted for the end of 2026, with Broadcom doing the silicon implementation and OpenAI's own models reportedly used to accelerate parts of the physical design and verification work.

Jalapeño is an inference accelerator, not a training chip. That distinction matters: inference — the cost of actually serving a model to a user every time someone hits enter on ChatGPT — is where OpenAI's variable costs scale linearly with usage. Early figures from the companies suggest Jalapeño delivers performance-per-watt 'substantially better' than current state-of-the-art, which in this market means Nvidia's GPUs. If those numbers hold in production, OpenAI's unit economics for serving GPT-5 and later models change meaningfully, and the company stops being a pure customer of Nvidia for its largest cost line.

The announcement fits a broader 2026 pattern in which the frontier labs are vertically integrating to escape GPU scarcity and GPU pricing. Anthropic has deepened its TPU bet with Google, xAI is building its own data centers, and now OpenAI has a credible first piece of its own silicon stack — paired in the same week with reports that the company is preparing to file confidentially for an IPO as soon as September. Owning more of the compute stack is exactly the kind of margin story a public-market roadshow benefits from.

A takeaway for learners: when you read about an AI lab launching its own chip, look for whether it's for training or inference, and at what stage of deployment. Training chips signal a long-term capability play; inference chips signal a near-term cost play. Both matter, but they tell you very different things about a company's product roadmap and its compute economics over the next 18 months.
